assertions.text: Output file is created at data/trade-journal/daily/YYYY-MM-DD/premarket.md with a valid date filename
Briefing header contains a US trading date in YYYY-MM-DD form
Briefing contains all 7 required section headings: '## 1.1', '## 1.2', '## 1.3', '## 1.4', '## Quick Summary', '## Market News & Catalysts', '## Global Market Spillover'
Section 1.1 includes an explicit RISK-ON or RISK-OFF verdict

Section 1.1 includes a Dollar/Yields regime classification — one of SCARED / GREEDY / GOLDILOCKS / NEUTRAL — with an evidenced prior-close → now direction for DXY and US10Y, and flags a MOOD/REGIME SPLIT when the regime contradicts the RISK-ON/OFF verdict.

Section 1.1 carries the four market-tape lines — '**Futures (implied open):**', '**Volatility:**' (VIX/VXN with a calm/normal/elevated/high tag), '**Sector tape:**', and '**Commodities:**' — sourced from the Step 3a browser tape pull. Each field is a real grepped number or an explicit 'N/A — <reason>'; never a guessed figure.
Section 1.2 either lists events with HIGH/MED/LOW impact labels, or states 'Light calendar today — no major data releases.'

Section 1.4 (Portfolio News) lists only watchlist tickers that have a news item or catalyst, grouped under the watchlist's theme sub-headers as bolded tokens **TICKER**; no 'Quiet today.' filler lines, and theme groups with no news are omitted.
Global Spillover section covers Asia (Nikkei, Hang Seng, KOSPI, Shanghai), Europe (DAX, FTSE, STOXX), and USD/JPY

evals.name: casual-trigger
evals.prompt: what should I watch in the market today

assertions.text: Skill is invoked (output follows the briefing template structure, not a freeform answer)
Section 1.4 (Portfolio News) reports only watchlist tickers with news or a catalyst, grouped by theme; quiet tickers are dropped

evals.name: indirect-trigger
evals.prompt: brief me on the upcoming US session

assertions.text: Computed US trading date is forward-looking (next session, not a past date) — verifiable by checking the date in the header against the eval run date
Briefing voice is plain English (no jargon like 'dot plot', 'hawkish bias', 'risk-parity rotation' without explanation)
